title: Lands of Galzyr: A Wonder of Design and Adventure
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AgcnylfczPE

After several more plays, Daniel (@dungeondive) returns with a full review of Lands of Galzyr — and he's in love. He calls it a marvel of design and execution, the game for people who've poured thousands of hours into Skyrim and never finished the main quest: an open-world critter-crawl of wandering, side quests, and charming random encounters in a beautifully realized world. For sense of adventure he ranks it with Sleeping Gods; for effortless fun, with Role Player Adventures and Familiar Tales.

Most of the review showcases the game's astonishing flexibility: drop-in/drop-out co-op, switch characters or player counts mid-campaign, swap between co-op and competitive — set up or saved in about a minute, the lowest barrier to entry imaginable. His one real complaint, possibly a dealbreaker for some, is that the *characters* barely change: because the game must stay balanced for drop-in play, there's rarely a guiding reason to alter your static skills or swap items. He proposes a house rule (banking card 101's respec for when it's needed) and wishes for seeded foreknowledge of upcoming challenges to make characters feel like they evolve. That aside, a delightful, near-top-10 recommendation for almost anyone.

----

Daniel's only real gripe with Lands of Galzyr is that its characters stay static so drop-in co-op stays balanced. Is that a fair trade for effortless accessibility, or do you need your hero to visibly grow?
